reasoning_model_1: The model accurately reproduces the text without any additions or omissions. The audio starts with a noticeable happy and enthusiastic tone ('I successfully published my first academic paper...'), characterized by a higher pitch and faster pace. At the conjunction 'But', the model effectively switches to a sadder, slower, and lower-pitched tone ('my advisor just told me...'), conveying disappointment. The transition is smooth and aligns perfectly with the semantic shift in the text.
reasoning_model_2: The model fails significantly in content accuracy. Instead of reading only the target script, it vocalizes instructional cues or hallucinations such as 'Stay surely happy tone' at the beginning and 'suddenly switches to a sad tone' in the middle of the speech. While there is some modulation in tone attempting to follow these spoken instructions, the inclusion of non-script text constitutes a major error in adhering to the user's command.
model_comparison: Model 1 followed the instructions perfectly, delivering the correct text with the requested emotional transition from happy to sad at the appropriate moment. Model 2 failed completely on content accuracy by reading out loud instructional cues ('Stay surely happy tone', 'suddenly switches to a sad tone') inserted into the script. Therefore, Model 1 is the clear winner due to Model 2's critical failure to separate instructions from the speech content.
